paragraphs.text: Dear people, I became aware of this Digital Fairness Act through the discussion about the ‘Stop killing games’ movement. Now, my comment is as follows: Unfair commercial practices related to dark patterns – The idea is good because these INFAI commercial practices exist on almost all websites and are now so bad that Bsi and other digital offices around the world strongly recommend the benefits of Ublock and other advertising blocking programs for browsers. Link: https://www.bsi.bund.de/DE/Themen/Verbraucherinnen-und-Verbraucher/Informationen-und-Empfehlungen/Cyber-Sicherheitsempfehlungen/cyber-sicherheitsempfehlungen_node.html 2.misleading marketing by influencers -Oftmals, the influencers have no reason or insufficient resources to distinguish problematic sponsors from non-problematic sponsors. At the same time, you must also look at your pay slips. Above all, I want my data to be protected from the outset and not used/abused for surveillance activities. The fact that this is the most money for the influencers should also be mentioned. In general, there is no system that rewards balanced decisions. And those who don’t have scrupuls get more money. Example of the Lord scam in Scotland in 2024, where allegedly ordinary people could buy a Lord title with land. Or the Lunchly, which published one of the Paul Brothers. Many food portions were smelled and did not meet any significant health standards. 3.addictive design of digital products – This happens everywhere on the internet and uses psychological tricks such as the Skinner Box method to get money from potential customers at all costs. GNP. E.g. https://www.faz.net/aktuell/ A more typical example is Gacha systems in games such as Genshin Impact or Battlepass systems in games such as Call of Duty, Fortnite or Valorant, which are designed to require more gaming hours from the player, otherwise you will not get a special ski or similar that you would like to receive. In addition, our data will then be sold back to third parties only in order to carry out our information-gathering service. E.g. https://www.faz.net/aktuell/ A more typical example is Gacha systems in games such as Genshin Impact or Battlepass systems in games such as Call of Duty, Fortnite or Valorant, which are designed to require more gaming hours from the player, otherwise you will not get a special ski or similar that you would like to receive. I am familiar with these systems under the term ‘FOMO’ (Fear of Missing Out) counter-examples to typical addictive design and FOMO would be the DRG (Deep Rock Galactic) and Helldivers 2 Battlepass. The battle passes remain forever and it is possible to obtain the premium currency in both games to an acceptable extent by playing hours. In the same vein, the Digital Fairness Act could also take action against videogames companies which require us to destroy their games after the end of the company’s support. It is a shame that most games – one part of our cultural expression – can often only obtain a licence to play. This is already a reminder of horror scenarios that recall George Orwell’s 1984 book. Now we still need to destroy our games? If this were to be required from publishers of books, there would be a public statement and public law would immediately discuss it! This means that video game manufacturers, just like the objective of your addictive design, do not have to compete with older good games and thus limit the user’s choice and play the new game if this player wants to have a similar experience. GNP: https://www.playstationinfo.de/2025/07/07/ubisoft-wegen-eula-klausel-unter-beschuss-spieler-sollen-ihre-games-zerstoeren-ist-das-ernst-gemeint/ 4.unfair personalisation practices. I have no constructive comment on this. It is determined that it happens, but I have not so far deliberately experienced it.
